english: I am staying inside today because the weather is too bad.
german: Ich bleibe heute drinnen, weil das Wetter zu schlecht ist.
grammar_note: A2 complexity requires joining clauses. The subordinating conjunction 'weil' (because) sends the conjugated verb 'ist' to the very end of the sentence.

english: Could you please tell me where the train station is?
german: Könnten Sie mir bitte sagen, wo der Bahnhof ist?
grammar_note: An indirect question. 'Könnten' is the polite subjunctive (Konjunktiv II). The question word 'wo' introduces a dependent clause, pushing 'ist' to the end.

english: I wanted to go shopping, but the stores were already closed.
german: Ich wollte einkaufen gehen, aber die Geschäfte waren schon geschlossen.
grammar_note: Uses the simple past (Präteritum) of a modal verb 'wollte'. The coordinating conjunction 'aber' doesn't affect word order (position zero).

english: If I have time tomorrow, I will help you move.
german: Wenn ich morgen Zeit habe, helfe ich dir beim Umzug.
grammar_note: A conditional 'wenn' clause. Because the entire 'wenn' clause acts as position 1 of the main sentence, the main verb 'helfe' must immediately follow the comma (position 2).

english: He told me that he is coming to the party later.
german: Er hat mir gesagt, dass er später zur Party kommt.
grammar_note: The conjunction 'dass' (that) introduces a subordinate clause, which requires the conjugated verb 'kommt' to sit at the absolute end.

english: I have to clean my apartment before my parents arrive.
german: Ich muss meine Wohnung aufräumen, bevor meine Eltern ankommen.
grammar_note: 'Bevor' is a subordinating conjunction. Note that even though 'ankommen' is a separable verb, it does not separate here because it is sent to the end of a subordinate clause.

english: We didn't go to the beach because it was raining all day.
german: Wir sind nicht zum Strand gegangen, weil es den ganzen Tag geregnet hat.
grammar_note: Combines a conversational past tense (Perfekt) movement phrase with a 'weil' clause. The auxiliary verb 'hat' goes to the very end, following the past participle.

english: I am looking forward to our trip next week.
german: Ich freue mich auf unsere Reise nächste Woche.
grammar_note: A2 introduces fixed verb-preposition combinations. 'Sich freuen auf' means looking forward to a future event and requires the accusative case ('unsere Reise').

english: Can you please turn off the light? I want to sleep.
german: Kannst du bitte das Licht ausmachen? Ich möchte schlafen.
grammar_note: Separable verb 'ausmachen' (to turn off). Since it is paired with the modal verb 'kannst', the entire infinitive 'ausmachen' goes to the end of the first clause.

english: My brother has found a new job in Hamburg.
german: Mein Bruder hat eine neue Stelle in Hamburg gefunden.
grammar_note: A2 expanding vocabulary: 'Stelle' or 'Arbeitsplatz' is more natural than the basic A1 'Job'. Adjective ending '-e' for feminine accusative singular.

english: I am interested in your project.
german: Ich interessiere mich für Ihr Projekt.
grammar_note: The reflexive phrase 'sich interessieren für' takes the accusative case. 'Ihr' is the formal capitalized possessive 'your'.

english: When I was young, I lived in a small village.
german: Als ich jung war, habe ich in einem kleinen Dorf gewohnt.
grammar_note: Use 'als' for single, continuous events in the past (when I was...). Like 'wenn', the comma causes verb-verb friction ('war, habe').

english: I think that this movie is very interesting.
german: Ich glaube, dass dieser Film sehr interessant ist.
grammar_note: An expression of opinion utilizing 'dass' to create an A2 subordinate structure. 'Dieser' is the demonstrative pronoun 'this' in masculine nominative.

english: I'm sorry, but I didn't understand your question.
german: Es tut mir leid, aber ich habe deine Frage nicht verstanden.
grammar_note: 'Verstehen' is an irregular verb whose past participle is 'verstanden' (no 'ge-' prefix because 'ver-' is an inseparable prefix).

english: Could you pass me the salt, please?
german: Könntest du mir bitte das Salz reichen?
grammar_note: Using the more sophisticated A2 verb 'reichen' (to pass/reach) instead of just 'geben' (to give). 'Mir' is dative, 'das Salz' is accusative.

english: We need to hurry up or we will miss the bus.
german: Wir müssen uns beeilen, sonst verpassen wir den Bus.
grammar_note: 'Sich beeilen' is a mandatory reflexive verb. 'Sonst' (otherwise) is an adverbial conjunction, meaning the verb 'verpassen' must come immediately after it.

english: I bought this shirt yesterday because it was on sale.
german: Ich habe dieses Hemd gestern gekauft, weil es im Angebot war.
grammar_note: 'Im Angebot' is the idiomatic way to say 'on sale'. The sentence showcases time-manner-place logic combined with a dependent past-tense clause.

english: What did you say? I wasn't listening closely.
german: Was hast du gesagt? Ich habe nicht genau zugehört.
grammar_note: The verb 'zuhören' means to listen actively/closely, as opposed to 'hören' (just hearing). It is separable, forming 'zugehört' in the past participle.

english: During the vacation, we want to travel to Switzerland.
german: In den Flitterwochen/In den Ferien wollen wir in die Schweiz reisen.
grammar_note: Switzerland ('die Schweiz') is one of the few countries that carries a mandatory feminine article. Movement 'into' it requires the accusative 'in die'.

english: My friend called me while I was cooking dinner.
german: Mein Freund hat mich angerufen, während ich das Abendessen gekocht habe.
grammar_note: 'Während' (while) is a subordinating conjunction that links two simultaneous past actions and requires the verb 'habe' at the end.

english: I am calling you to invite you to my birthday.
german: Ich rufe dich an, um dich zu meinem Geburtstag einzuladen.
grammar_note: An 'um... zu' infinitive clause expressing purpose ('in order to'). The 'zu' splits the separable prefix of the verb: 'einzuladen'.

english: You should drink less coffee if you cannot sleep.
german: Du solltest weniger Kaffee trinken, wenn du nicht schlafen kannst.
grammar_note: 'Solltest' is the Konjunktiv II advice form ('should'). The subordinator 'wenn' forces the modal verb 'kannst' to the very end of its clause.

english: The keys are lying on the brown wooden table.
german: Die Schlüssel liegen auf dem braunen Holztisch.
grammar_note: A2 moves to compound nouns ('Holztisch') and mixed adjective endings. 'Auf' indicates static location here, triggering dative case ('dem braunen').

english: I am putting the keys on the round table.
german: Ich lege die Schlüssel auf den runden Tisch.
grammar_note: Contrast with the previous card: 'legen' indicates directional movement, forcing the preposition 'auf' to take the accusative case ('den runden').

english: Although it was cold, we went for a walk in the park.
german: Obwohl es kalt war, sind wir im Park spazieren gegangen.
grammar_note: 'Obwohl' (although) is a subordinating conjunction. When the subordinate clause starts the sentence, the main clause must start with its verb ('sind').

english: I don't know yet if I can come tomorrow.
german: Ich weiß noch nicht, ob ich morgen kommen kann.
grammar_note: 'Ob' translates to 'whether/if' in yes-or-no questions. It is a subordinating conjunction, sending the modal verb 'kann' to the end.

english: The doctor advised me to drink more water.
german: Der Arzt hat mir geraten, mehr Wasser zu trinken.
grammar_note: An infinitive clause with 'zu'. The main verb 'raten' triggers a dative object ('mir') and sets up a description of the advice at the end.

english: We have been living in this beautiful city for five years.
german: Wir wohnen seit fünf Jahren in dieser schönen Stadt.
grammar_note: 'Seit' (since/for) requires present tense in German if the action is ongoing. It also triggers the dative case for 'Jahren' and 'dieser schönen Stadt'.

english: I would like to make an appointment for Tuesday.
german: Ich möchte einen Termin für Dienstag vereinbaren.
grammar_note: A2 workplace/survival skill: 'einen Termin vereinbaren' is the standard professional collocation for making an appointment.

english: Can you tell me how much this jacket costs?
german: Können Sie mir sagen, wie viel diese Jacke kostet?
grammar_note: An embedded indirect question. The question phrase 'wie viel' acts as the subordinator, sending 'kostet' to the end.

english: He ran quickly to the bus stop so he wouldn't be late.
german: Er ist schnell zur Bushaltestelle gelaufen, damit er sich nicht verspätet.
grammar_note: 'Damit' (so that / in order that) is used when the subject of the purpose clause is different or explicitly stated, functioning as a subordinator.

english: I am satisfied with my new apartment.
german: Ich bin mit meiner neuen Wohnung zufrieden.
grammar_note: The adjective 'zufrieden' pairs with the preposition 'mit' (satisfied with). 'Mit' always dictates the dative case.

english: Could you please repeat that slower?
german: Könnten Sie das bitte langsamer wiederholen?
grammar_note: Uses the comparative form of the adjective 'langsam' ('langsamer') inside a polite conditional request framework.

english: I have to work late today, so I cannot come.
german: Ich muss heute lange arbeiten, deshalb kann ich nicht kommen.
grammar_note: 'Deshalb' (therefore/that's why) is a connector that occupies position 1, forcing the verb 'kann' to sit in position 2 right after it.

english: My laptop broke down last week.
german: Mein Laptop ist letzte Woche kaputtgegangen.
grammar_note: The compound verb 'kaputtgehen' uses 'sein' as its auxiliary verb in the past tense because it denotes a change of state.

english: I apply for the position as an accountant.
german: Ich bewerbe mich um die Stelle als Buchhalter.
grammar_note: A2 professional skill: 'Sich bewerben um' (to apply for) is a mandatory reflexive verb taking an accusative object.

english: The dress is pretty, but it doesn't fit me.
german: Das Kleid ist schön, aber es passt mir nicht.
grammar_note: The verb 'passen' (to fit physically) requires the dative case for the person being fitted ('mir').

english: We got lost because we didn't have a map with us.
german: Wir haben uns verlaufen, weil wir keinen Stadtplan dabei hatten.
grammar_note: 'Sich verlaufen' is getting lost on foot. 'Dabeihaben' is a compound concept meaning to have something on your person.

english: I am sending you the documents via email.
german: Ich schicke Ihnen die Dokumente per E-Mail.
grammar_note: Standard business communication sentence. 'Ihnen' is formal dative 'to you'; 'die Dokumente' is plural accusative.

english: He asks his colleague for help.
german: Er bittet seinen Kollegen um Hilfe.
grammar_note: The verb 'bitten' requires an accusative person ('seinen Kollegen' n-declension) followed by the preposition 'um' + accusative item.

english: I would like to cancel my reservation.
german: Ich möchte meine Reservierung stornieren.
grammar_note: A2 travel German: Moving beyond 'absagen' to the specific commercial verb 'stornieren' (to cancel a booking/contract).

english: The car belongs to my older cousin.
german: Das Auto gehört meinem älteren Cousin.
grammar_note: The verb 'gehören' (to belong to) always triggers the dative case for its owner object ('meinem älteren').

english: She is proud of her successful daughter.
german: Sie ist stolz auf ihre erfolgreiche Tochter.
grammar_note: The fixed prepositional adjective phrase 'stolz auf' requires the accusative case. 'Erfolgreiche' shows feminine descriptive ending.

english: Please remember to lock the door.
german: Bitte denk daran, die Tür abzuschließen.
grammar_note: Uses the da-compound 'daran' to anticipate the infinitive clause. 'Abschließen' is a separable verb split by 'zu'.

english: I prefer drinking tea instead of coffee.
german: Ich trinke lieber Tee statt Kaffee.
grammar_note: 'Lieber' is the comparative form of 'gern', used to show preference. 'Statt' acts as a preposition meaning instead of.

english: The train leaves from platform four.
german: Der Zug fährt von Gleis vier ab.
grammar_note: The verb is the separable 'abfahren' (to depart). The prefix 'ab' must stand at the very end of the main independent clause.

english: I spent my childhood in Austria.
german: Ich habe meine Kindheit in Österreich verbracht.
grammar_note: The past tense of 'verbringen' (to spend time) is irregular: 'verbracht'. It does not use 'ge-' because of the 'ver-' prefix.

english: He changed his mind at the last moment.
german: Er hat sich im letzten Moment umentschieden.
grammar_note: The reflexive verb 'sich umentscheiden' means to alter one's choice or plan. 'Im letzten Moment' utilizes dative masculine time structure.

english: We are getting married next month.
german: Wir heiraten nächsten Monat.
grammar_note: Time expressions without a preposition ('nächsten Monat') default to the accusative case in German.

english: I need to open a bank account as soon as possible.
german: Ich muss so schnell wie möglich ein Bankkonto eröffnen.
grammar_note: 'So schnell wie möglich' is a fixed idiom for 'as fast as possible'. 'Eröffnen' is a more advanced A2 verb for opening an account/business.

english: The heating isn't working properly in my room.
german: Die Heizung funktioniert in meinem Zimmer nicht richtig.
grammar_note: 'Funktionieren' is preferred over 'arbeiten' for mechanical/digital devices. 'Nicht' negates the adverb 'richtig' and goes late.

english: Can you recommend a good restaurant near here?
german: Können Sie ein gutes Restaurant in der Nähe empfehlen?
grammar_note: 'Empfehlen' is an irregular verb. In this sentence, 'ein gutes Restaurant' carries the mixed adjective endings for a neuter object.

english: I am getting used to the cold weather gradually.
german: Ich gewöhne mich langsam an das kalte Wetter.
grammar_note: 'Sich gewöhnen an' (to get used to) is a reflexive verb combo that dictates the accusative case for the object of your habituation.

english: It is important that you sign the contract today.
german: Es ist wichtig, dass Sie den Vertrag heute unterschreiben.
grammar_note: A complex construction. 'Es ist wichtig' acts as an introductory framework triggering a subordinate 'dass' clause with the verb placed last.

english: I used to play soccer every week, but now I don't have time.
german: Früher habe ich jede Woche Fußball gespielt, aber jetzt habe ich keine Zeit.
grammar_note: 'Früher' (earlier/used to) sits in position 1, forcing inversion with the auxiliary verb 'habe'. This creates a clear past/present contrast.

english: The apartment is located on the third floor.
german: Die Wohnung liegt im dritten Stock.
grammar_note: Germans use ordinal numbers for floors, which require adjective declension after the hidden dative preposition 'im' (in dem).

english: I would like to complain about the loud noise.
german: Ich möchte mich über den lauten Lärm beschweren.
grammar_note: The reflexive verb 'sich beschweren über' (to complain about) demands an accusative target ('den lauten Lärm').

english: She came home late because she missed the last tram.
german: Sie ist spät nach Hause gekommen, weil sie die letzte Straßenbahn verpasst hat.
grammar_note: 'Straßenbahn' is an A2 level city vocabulary word. The 'weil' structure forces the auxiliary verb 'hat' past the participle 'verpasst'.

english: We must discuss this problem together.
german: Wir müssen dieses Problem zusammen besprechen.
grammar_note: 'Besprechen' implies a deeper, more constructive discussion than the basic A1 verb 'sprechen'.

english: My parents are visiting us over the weekend.
german: Meine Eltern besuchen uns am Wochenende.
grammar_note: 'Besuchen' acts directly on the accusative plural pronoun 'uns'. Time component 'am Wochenende' takes standard position.

english: I forgot my wallet, so I have to go back.
german: Ich habe meinen Geldbeutel vergessen, deshalb muss ich zurückgehen.
grammar_note: 'Geldbeutel' is a standard A2 word for wallet. 'Deshalb' transitions into an inverted clause structure ('muss ich').

english: Can you tell me how I can reach you?
german: Können Sie mir sagen, wie ich Sie erreichen kann?
grammar_note: Indirect question formatting. 'Erreichen' (to reach/contact) sits as an infinitive ahead of the closing conjugated modal 'kann'.

english: I am looking for a quiet place where I can work.
german: Ich suche einen ruhigen Ort, wo ich arbeiten kann.
grammar_note: A2 relative-style location clause. 'Einen ruhigen Ort' showcases the masculine accusative adjective suffix '-en'.

english: The food tastes excellent today.
german: Das Essen schmeckt heute hervorragend.
grammar_note: Upgrading vocabulary from simple 'gut' or 'lecker' to 'hervorragend' (excellent/outstanding).

english: I'm sorry, I'm not familiar with this area.
german: Entschuldigung, ich kenne mich in dieser Gegend nicht aus.
grammar_note: The idiom 'sich auskennen' means to know your way around or be familiar with something. It splits around the dative location phrase.

english: We are planning to rent a car for our trip.
german: Wir planen, für unsere Reise ein Auto zu mieten.
grammar_note: An infinitive phrase utilizing 'zu mieten' wrapped neatly around the direct accusative object of the intention.

english: He has changed his phone number again.
german: Er hat seine Telefonnummer schon wieder geändert.
grammar_note: 'Schon wieder' is an idiomatic filler meaning 'yet again'. 'Geändert' is the past participle of regular verb 'ändern'.

english: The course starts at the beginning of October.
german: Der Kurs beginnt Anfang Oktober.
grammar_note: 'Anfang' followed directly by a month is a common time shorthand omitting the need for complex genitive structures at A2.

english: I cannot come because I have caught a cold.
german: Ich kann nicht kommen, weil ich mich erkältet habe.
grammar_note: The verb 'sich erkälten' is reflexive. In the subordinate clause, the auxiliary 'habe' moves behind the reflexive pronoun and past participle.

english: Could you please fill out this form?
german: Könnten Sie bitte dieses Formular ausfüllen?
grammar_note: A2 bureaucratic/functional literacy. 'Ausfüllen' is a separable verb meaning to fill out a document completely.

english: The supermarket is right opposite the post office.
german: Der Supermarkt liegt direkt gegenüber der Post.
grammar_note: The preposition 'gegenüber' always requires the dative case, changing the feminine 'die Post' to 'der Post'.

english: I want to improve my German skills this year.
german: Ich möchte dieses Jahr meine Deutschkenntnisse verbessern.
grammar_note: 'Deutschkenntnisse' (German knowledge/skills) is an expansive plural noun. 'Verbessern' is the infinitive pushed to the end.

english: The weather was so beautiful that we stayed outside until late at night.
german: Das Wetter war so schön, dass wir bis spät in die Nacht draußen geblieben sind.
grammar_note: Conjunction 'dass' (so... that) creates a subordinate frame. 'Bleiben' indicates a status but takes 'sein' (*sind*) in past perfect.

english: I received an invitation to the wedding.
german: Ich habe eine Einladung zur Hochzeit erhalten.
grammar_note: Upgrading from the simple A1 'bekommen' to the proper A2 'erhalten' (to receive). 'Zur' contracts 'zu der' (dative feminine).

english: My coworkers are very helpful and friendly.
german: Meine Kollegen sind sehr hilfsbereit und freundlich.
grammar_note: 'Hilfsbereit' (ready to help) is a crucial A2 compound adjective for workplace and descriptive profiles.

english: I forgot my password, so I cannot log in.
german: Ich habe mein Passwort vergessen, sodass ich mich nicht anmelden kann.
grammar_note: 'Sodass' introduces a consecutive clause showing result, acting as a subordinator that leaves 'kann' at the very tail.

english: The pharmacy is closed on Sundays.
german: Die Apotheke ist sonntags geschlossen.
grammar_note: Adding a lowercase '-s' to a day of the week creates an adverb meaning 'every Sunday' or 'on Sundays' generally.

english: I have to return this book to the library today.
german: Ich muss dieses Buch heute der Bibliothek zurückgeben.
grammar_note: The separable verb 'zurückgeben' requires a dative recipient ('der Bibliothek') and an accusative object ('dieses Buch').

english: He speaks German fluently although he has only been learning it for a year.
german: Er spricht fließend Deutsch, obwohl er es erst seit einem Jahr lernt.
grammar_note: Vocabulary expansion: 'fließend' (fluently). 'Erst' is used instead of 'nur' when discussing time durations to mean 'only so far'.

english: We must protect our environment.
german: Wir müssen unsere Umwelt schützen.
grammar_note: Environmental topics enter at A2. 'Schützen' is a regular transitive verb paired with the accusative plural/feminine possessive.

english: Could you help me move next Saturday?
german: Könntest du mir nächsten Samstag beim Umzug helfen?
grammar_note: The formula 'helfen bei' means to help with something. 'Bei' collapses with 'dem' to form 'beim', requiring the dative noun.

english: I am applying for a visa at the embassy.
german: Ich beantrage ein Visum bei der Botschaft.
grammar_note: 'Beantragen' is a non-separable verb meaning to formally apply for a document. 'Bei' indicates the institution you are dealing with.

english: The museum offers a discount for students.
german: Das Museum bietet einen Rabatt für Studenten.
grammar_note: 'Bieten' is the base verb for offering. 'Rabatt' is masculine accusative, receiving the suffix '-en' from the indefinite article.

english: I have broken my leg and cannot walk.
german: Ich habe mir das Bein gebrochen und kann nicht gehen.
grammar_note: Uses a reflexive dative structure ('mir das Bein gebrochen') because breaking your own body part is viewed as an event that happens to you.

english: The flight was cancelled due to the strike.
german: Der Flug wurde wegen des Streiks annulliert.
grammar_note: An introduction to the real passive voice structure ('wurde... annulliert') combined with the preposition 'wegen' which technically prefers genitive.

english: I am getting used to the food here slowly.
german: Ich gewöhne mich langsam an das Essen hier.
grammar_note: Reinforcing the A2 reflexive pattern 'sich gewöhnen an' + accusative case.

english: The customer requested a refund.
german: Der Kunde hat eine Rückerstattung verlangt.
grammar_note: Advanced customer interaction vocabulary. 'Verlangen' means to demand or formally request; 'Rückerstattung' is a refund.

english: I spent the weekend with my grandparents in the countryside.
german: Ich habe das Wochenende mit meinen Großeltern auf dem Land verbracht.
grammar_note: 'Auf dem Land' is the fixed idiomatic expression for being located out in the countryside (dative case).

english: The view from the mountain was breathtaking.
german: Die Aussicht vom Berg war atemberaubend.
grammar_note: Compound word mastery: 'atemberaubend' (breath-robbing/breathtaking). 'Vom' contracts 'von dem'.

english: I cannot participate in the meeting tomorrow.
german: Ich kann morgen nicht am Meeting teilnehmen.
grammar_note: The separable verb 'teilnehmen an' means to participate in. It requires the dative case, contracting to 'am Meeting'.

english: Please send me a confirmation as soon as possible.
german: Bitte senden Sie mir so schnell wie möglich eine Bestätigung.
grammar_note: Formal workplace phrasing. 'Bestätigung' (confirmation) is a typical A2 professional communication noun.

english: I left my umbrella in the subway.
german: Ich habe meinen Regenschirm in der U-Bahn liegen lassen.
grammar_note: Using 'liegen lassen' (to leave something behind accidentally). In past tense compound structures, it uses a double infinitive effect.

english: He left the room without saying a word.
german: Er hat den Raum verlassen, ohne ein Wort zu sagen.
grammar_note: An 'ohne... zu' infinitive construction meaning 'without doing something'. The phrase frames the action cleanly at the end.

english: I am looking for a flatmate who can cook well.
german: Ich suche einen Mitbewohner, der gut kochen kann.
grammar_note: Introduction of basic relative clauses. The relative pronoun 'der' matches the masculine 'Mitbewohner' and sends 'kann' to the back.

english: The printer is out of paper again.
german: Der Drucker hat schon wieder kein Papier mehr.
grammar_note: 'Kein... mehr' is the idiomatic standard for 'no more' or 'out of' a physical resource.

english: We need to cancel our flight due to an emergency.
german: Wir müssen unseren Flug wegen eines Notfalls stornieren.
grammar_note: 'Wegen eines Notfalls' shows proper masculine genitive modification of the indefinite article and noun suffix ('-s').

english: I prefer living in a quiet neighborhood.
german: Ich wohne lieber in einer ruhigen Nachbarschaft.
grammar_note: Using 'lieber' embedded next to the verb to signify structural preference in an apartment search context.

english: The traffic light turned red just as I arrived.
german: Die Ampel wurde rot, als ich gerade angekommen bin.
grammar_note: 'Werden' in its simple past form 'wurde' signifies a transformation or change of state (the light turned red).

english: He passed his driver's license exam yesterday.
german: Er hat gestern seine Führerscheinprüfung bestanden.
grammar_note: A2 compounding: 'Führerscheinprüfung'. The verb 'bestehen' (to pass an exam) forms its past participle without a 'ge-' prefix.

english: I would like to extend my lease if possible.
german: Ich möchte meinen Mietvertrag verlängern, wenn es möglich ist.
grammar_note: Essential tenant communication phrasing. 'Mietvertrag' is lease/rental contract; 'verlängern' means to extend or lengthen.

english: She fell asleep while she was reading a book.
german: Sie ist eingeschlafen, während sie ein Buch gelesen hat.
grammar_note: The past tense of the change-of-state verb 'einschlafen' requires the auxiliary 'sein' (*ist*), paired with a simultaneous 'während' clause.

english: The machine stops automatically if an error occurs.
german: Die Maschine stoppt automatisch, wenn ein Fehler auftritt.
grammar_note: A2 technical concept. The verb 'auftreten' is strong/irregular and changes its vowel stem ('tritt... auf') in the third person singular.

english: I have to share an office with a coworker.
german: Ich muss mir ein Büro mit einem Kollegen teilen.
grammar_note: The verb 'teilen' utilizes a reflexive dative pronoun ('mir') when you mean you are sharing something for your own utility.

english: He acts as if he knows everything.
german: Er tut so, als ob er alles wüsste.
grammar_note: A useful idiomatic structure 'tun als ob' (to act as if), which pushes the verb to the end in its subjunctive state.

english: I need to exchange these shoes because they are too tight.
german: Ich muss diese Schuhe umtauschen, weil sie zu eng sind.
grammar_note: Commercial skill: 'Umtauschen' means specifically to swap an item at a retail counter. 'Eng' expands on descriptions past simple color/shape.

english: The elevator is out of order, so we must take the stairs.
german: Der Aufzug ist außer Betrieb, deshalb müssen wir die Treppe nehmen.
grammar_note: 'Außer Betrieb' is the professional idiomatic equivalent to 'out of order' for heavy machinery/infrastructure.

english: I am register myself for the advanced course.
german: Ich melde mich für den Fortgeschrittenenkurs an.
grammar_note: The separable reflexive verb 'sich anmelden für' means to register for an event, directing accusative case layout.

english: We checked into the hotel late last night.
german: Wir haben gestern Spätbühne im Hotel eingecheckt.
grammar_note: Germans have adopted the global travel verb 'einchecken', forming its participle cleanly as 'eingecheckt'.

english: I have a package that I need to drop off at the post office.
german: Ich habe ein Paket, das ich bei der Post abgeben muss.
grammar_note: A relative clause introduced by the neuter relative pronoun 'das', which terminates with the closing combination of infinitive and modal.

english: The project must be finished by tomorrow.
german: Das Projekt muss bis morgen fertiggestellt sein.
grammar_note: Using 'fertigstellen' instead of the simple adjective 'fertig'. This uses a passive state construction at the absolute finish.

english: I don't mind if it takes a bit longer.
german: Es macht mir nichts aus, wenn es etwas länger dauert.
grammar_note: The phrase 'jemandem etwas ausmachen' translates to minding or being bothered by something. It separates neatly here.

english: The contract was signed by both parties.
german: Der Vertrag wurde von beiden Parteien unterschrieben.
grammar_note: A clear past passive sentence using 'wurde' combined with the agent marker 'von' which demands a plural dative ending ('beiden').

english: I am not allowed to eat sweets because of my diet.
german: Ich darf wegen meiner Diät keine Süßigkeiten essen.
grammar_note: Modal verb 'dürfen' denotes permission/prohibition. Paired with 'wegen' which alters the possessive to dative/genitive context.

english: Could you please recommend a reliable craftsman?
german: Könnten Sie mir bitte einen zuverlässigen Handwerker empfehlen?
grammar_note: A2 lifestyle situation. 'Zuverlässig' (reliable) shows proper masculine accusative adjective tailoring ('-en').

english: The noise outside prevents me from concentrating.
german: Der Lärm draußen hält mich davon ab, mich zu konzentrieren.
grammar_note: The compound framework 'jemanden abhalten von' means to prevent someone from doing something, combined with reflexive 'sich konzentrieren'.

english: I am transferring the money to your account right now.
german: Ich überwiese das Geld jetzt auf Ihr Konto.
grammar_note: Banking literacy: 'Überweisen' is to transfer funds digitally. The directional action onto an account triggers accusative case.

english: We had a lot of fun despite the continuous rain.
german: Wir hatten trotz des Dauerregens viel Spaß.
grammar_note: The sophisticated preposition 'trotz' (despite) requires the genitive case, leading to the modified masculine article 'des'.

english: I need a receipt to get my money back.
german: Ich brauche einen Beleg, um mein Geld zurückzubekommen.
grammar_note: 'Beleg' or 'Quittung' replaces basic A1 'Rechnung'. The sentence concludes with a multi-part 'um... zu' separation syntax.

english: He apologized for his rude behavior.
german: Er hat sich für sein unhöfliches Verhalten entschuldigt.
grammar_note: The reflexive pattern 'sich entschuldigen für' connects with an accusative object representing the cause of regret.

english: The event takes place in the city center.
german: Die Veranstaltung findet im Stadtzentrum statt.
grammar_note: Upgrading vocabulary from simple 'Party' to 'Veranstaltung' (event). The verb is the separable 'stattfinden'.

english: I am not sure if the store is open on Saturdays.
german: Ich bin mir nicht sicher, ob das Geschäft samstags geöffnet ist.
grammar_note: 'Ich bin mir sicher' uses a reflexive dative pronoun to show deep personal conviction or doubt relative to an 'ob' clause.

english: My grandfather tells stories about the old times frequently.
german: Mein Großvater erzählt oft Geschichten über die alten Zeiten.
grammar_note: 'Erzählen' goes beyond basic 'sagen' to imply structured narration or story-telling. Plural accusative adjective formatting follows.

english: I have decided to apply for the international scholarship.
german: Ich habe mich entschieden, mich um das internationale Stipendium zu bewerben.
grammar_note: A double reflexive display: 'sich entscheiden' (to decide) paired into an infinitive statement of 'sich bewerben um'.

english: The signal is very weak here inside the building.
german: Der Empfang ist hier drinnen im Gebäude sehr schwach.
grammar_note: Mobile connectivity literacy: 'Empfang' means reception/signal quality within spatial boundaries.

english: We should schedule a meeting to resolve the issue.
german: Wir sollten ein Treffen vereinbaren, um das Problem zu lösen.
grammar_note: Professional resolution structure utilizing 'sollten' paired with an 'um... zu' objective infinitive block.

english: I want to unsubscribe from this newsletter.
german: Ich möchte mich von diesem Newsletter abmelden.
grammar_note: Digital literacy: 'Sich abmelden von' means to sign out or unsubscribe from a content feed via dative connection.

english: The delivery was delayed by several days.
german: Die Lieferung hat sich um einige Tage verzögert.
grammar_note: Commercial context: 'Lieferung' (delivery). The reflexive verb 'sich verzögern' explains that a process ran late independently.

english: Could you please double-check the figures?
german: Könnten Sie die Zahlen bitte noch einmal überprüfen.
grammar_note: Office setting request form. 'Überprüfen' means to audit or verify rather than just look at something superficially.

english: I always separate plastics from paper waste.
german: Ich trenne Plastik immer vom Papiermüll.
grammar_note: A2 cultural standard topic: waste sorting. 'Trennen von' means to physically split or categorize items.

english: He promised me that he would keep the secret.
german: Er hat mir versprochen, dass er das Geheimnis für sich behält.
grammar_note: 'Versprechen' takes a dative recipient ('mir'). The subordinate clause wraps around the idiom 'für sich behalten' (keep to oneself).

english: The product is unfortunately no longer available in our stock.
german: Das Produkt ist leider nicht mehr in unserem Lager verfügbar.
grammar_note: Retail tracking speech. 'Verfügbar' (available) and 'Lager' (warehouse stock) represent solid A2 commercial mastery.

english: I am proud that I successfully completed the language level.
german: Ich bin stolz darauf, dass ich das Sprachniveau erfolgreich bestanden habe.
grammar_note: Links the placeholder 'darauf' to an explanatory subordinate clause summarizing structural accomplishment.

english: Please do not forget to attach the document to the email.
german: Bitte vergiss nicht, das Dokument an die E-Mail anzuhängen.
grammar_note: Digital attachment instruction. The verb is 'anhängen', which splits cleanly around the infinitive marker 'zu'.

english: The heating turns on automatically when temperature drops.
german: Die Heizung schaltet sich automatisch ein, wenn die Temperatur sinkt.
grammar_note: The technical verb is reflexive 'sich einschalten' (to activate itself), reacting directly to a conditional ambient change.

english: I would like to trade my old smartphone for a newer model.
german: Ich möchte mein altes Smartphone gegen ein neueres Modell eintauschen.
grammar_note: 'Eintauschen gegen' requires accusative transformation, combined with a comparative adjective ending ('neueres').

english: We must reduce our energy consumption to save costs.
german: Wir müssen unseren Energieverbrauch senken, um Kosten zu sparen.
grammar_note: Compound utility term: 'Energieverbrauch'. The purpose clause demonstrates efficient verb bundling at the close.

english: The doctor checked my blood pressure and told me to rest.
german: Der Arzt hat meinen Blutdruck gemessen und mir gesagt, dass ich mich ausruhen soll.
grammar_note: Combines irregular past participle 'gemessen' with an internal command framed via a polite subordinate modal constraint.

english: The application deadline ends at midnight.
german: Die Bewerbungsfrist endet um Mitternacht.
grammar_note: Administrative A2 vocabulary item: 'Bewerbungsfrist' (formal application timeline limit).

english: I am looking for an accommodation that allows pets.
german: Ich suche eine Unterkunft, die Haustiere erlaubt.
grammar_note: Upgrading from 'Wohnung' to generic 'Unterkunft' (accommodation) paired with a relative clause governing plural structures.

english: He cancelled the appointment because an unexpected problem arose.
german: Er hat den Termin abgesagt, weil ein unerwartetes Problem aufgetreten ist.
grammar_note: Showcases complex compound adjective modification ('unerwartetes') alongside an introspective irregular conversational past layout.

english: I try to eat healthier by cooking fresh food every day.
german: Ich versuche, mich gesünder zu ernähren, indem ich jeden Tag frisch koche.
grammar_note: Uses the conditional/modal connector 'indem' (by doing so), which tracks structural methodology and acts as a subordinator.

english: The warning signs were positioned clearly along the road.
german: Die Warnschilder waren deutlich entlang der Straße platziert.
grammar_note: The preposition 'entlang' often dictates genitive context when preceding a noun, leading to the feminine article layout 'der'.

english: I cannot download this attachment because the file is corrupted.
german: Ich kann diesen Anhang nicht herunterladen, weil die Datei beschädigt ist.
grammar_note: IT communication phrasing: 'Anhang' (attachment), 'herunterladen' (download) and 'beschädigt' (damaged/corrupted).

english: We need to replace the old batteries in the remote control.
german: Wir müssen die alten Batterien in der Fernbedienung austauschen.
grammar_note: Everyday home life terminology: 'Fernbedienung' (remote control) and the complex structural verb 'austauschen'.

english: The invoice includes all shipping costs.
german: Die Rechnung enthält alle Versandkosten.
grammar_note: Commercial standard phrase. 'Enthalten' is a strong verb (contains), paired with plural compound noun 'Versandkosten'.

english: I filled out the request form carefully.
german: Ich habe das Antragsformular sorgfältig ausgefüllt.
grammar_note: Improves vocabulary by shifting the adverb choice from simple 'gut' to the descriptive 'sorgfältig' (carefully/painstakingly).

english: The light turns on automatically as soon as someone enters the room.
german: Das Licht geht automatisch an, sobald jemand den Raum betritt.
grammar_note: The subordinator 'sobald' (as soon as) controls timing sequence and commands the irregular verb 'betritt' to hold the end stance.

english: I need to withdraw cash from the ATM before we eat.
german: Ich muss noch Geld am Geldautomaten abheben, bevor wir essen.
grammar_note: Financial functional skill: 'Geld abheben' (to withdraw money). 'Geldautomat' is the correct term for an ATM.

english: He explained to me how the new software functions.
german: Er hat mir erklärt, wie die neue Software funktioniert.
grammar_note: The main verb 'erklären' requires a dative object ('mir') indicating who received the description, opening up an indirect clause.

english: I look forward to hearing from you soon.
german: Ich freue mich darauf, bald von Ihnen zu hören.
grammar_note: The formal standard close for professional letters and emails, utilizing the da-compound placeholder 'darauf' ahead of an infinitive clause.
